project.title: Burke y Hare: cadáveres por dinero

visual.image_prompt: CLEAR LITERAL SCENE — it must SHOW a covered anatomy table in a candlelit room while coins fall beside a tagged bundle and a shovel is crossed out on the floor; a 1828 Edinburgh anatomy classroom with wooden benches, anatomy charts, candles, stone walls, foggy window and shadowy old-town rooftops behind; forbidden trade, chilling hook mood. Rich painterly 2D cartoon illustration with soft shading and textured brushwork, hand-painted look, bold clean outlines, soft natural light. NOT 3D, NOT Pixar, NOT dark, NOT cluttered. Funny minimalist white stick-figure people: round off-white heads, thin black stick limbs, simple oval bodies, big round expressive eyes, exaggerated comic faces. NOT animals. HERO RULE: the white stick-figure characters are LARGE in the foreground and the clear main subject, big and prominent with expressive faces; the setting is fully detailed BEHIND and around them with depth and props, but stays smaller than the characters and never becomes the main subject. RICH ENVIRONMENT: fill the frame with a real, specific, detailed setting with depth (clear foreground, midground and background), props and atmospheric light — NEVER a flat empty color wash behind the characters. CHARACTERIZE BY CULTURE: every human stick-figure is dressed for its role so the viewer instantly reads who they are, while KEEPING the round white stick-figure head, big eyes, NO hair, NO beard. William Burke: worn 1820s Edinburgh lodging-house clothes, patched brown coat, dark waistcoat, flat cap, restless greedy expression. William Hare: worn dark waistcoat, rough coat, crooked cap, sly nervous expression, often near Burke but slightly behind him. Robert Knox: respected private anatomy teacher in a black frock coat, white cravat, spectacles, lecture pointer, medical notes, composed expression, NEVER shown ordering murder. Anatomy students and assistants: dark 1820s suits, white aprons, notebooks, anatomy charts, candles and covered teaching tables. Vulnerable lodgers and poor residents: worn shawls, caps, patched coats, small travel bundles, tired or trusting expressions. Resurrection men and grave robbers: dark coats, lanterns, shovels, sacks and graveyard props. Police and courtroom figures: constables in dark coats and top hats, judge in black robe and white collar, executioner only as a distant silhouette. Edinburgh townspeople: 1820s coats, bonnets, shawls, market baskets and shocked faces. The narrator / a generic onlooker stays a plain white stick-figure with NO costume. Distinguish roles by costume. EVERY character is HIGHLY EXPRESSIVE and DYNAMIC: an exaggerated emotion and an active pose, never stiff; often show several stick-figures and elements interacting, not one lone figure; play with color by the moment but keep it tasteful, NOT oversaturated; make each scene feel different from the last. Tasteful and NON-GRAPHIC: convey violence or death ONLY through stylized suggestion — silhouettes, simplified stick-figures with simple X-marks for eyes, still figures, stakes, shadows, crows, smoke, dark landscape and distant shapes. NEVER explicit gore, blood, organs or realistic wounds. Suggest clearly, never show crudely. Palette: candle gold, ink black, old wood brown and cold Edinburgh blue. Camera: LOW-ANGLE CLOSE-UP. a bold hand-painted Spanish title reads "CADÁVERES POR DINERO" in clean bold flat letters with a big red arrow pointing to the central subject, NOT ornate, NOT gold-edged. SIMPLE UNCLUTTERED composition with generous negative space, readable at a glance, NOT busy. VERTICAL 9:16 short-video framing, subject centered with room for big text. Vertical 9:16, full-bleed. All text in clear bold Spanish exactly as written.

voiceover.text: [curious] No saqueaban tumbas.

tts_export.routing_note: Reel vertical menor a cinco mil caracteres: usar eleven_v3 con tags simples y pausas de texto, sin SSML.
tts_export.pronunciation_notes: Burke: Burk. Hare: Jer o Hair, elegir pronunciación consistente. Knox: Nox. Robert Knox: Róbert Nox. Edimburgo: E-dim-bur-go. Anatomía: a-na-to-mí-a.
tts_export.full_script: [curious] No saqueaban tumbas. Elegían a personas vivas, las asesinaban… y vendían sus cuerpos para enseñar anatomía. En mil ochocientos veintiocho, Edimburgo era un centro de enseñanza médica. Sus anatomistas necesitaban más cadáveres de los que podían obtener legalmente. Eso alimentó un mercado clandestino de cuerpos robados. William Burke y William Hare empezaron con el cadáver de un huésped que murió naturalmente. Luego dieron el salto: no esperarían a que alguien muriera. Ellos mismos provocarían la muerte. [appalled] Sus víctimas solían ser vulnerables: huéspedes, personas pobres, gente cuya ausencia podía tardar en despertar sospechas. Las emborrachaban, las asfixiaban y llevaban los cuerpos frescos a la escuela del anatomista Robert Knox. A cambio, recibían dinero. Al menos dieciséis víctimas. [thoughtful] El caso revela un sistema donde los cadáveres eran escasos, valiosos y difíciles de rastrear. No existe evidencia concluyente de que Knox supiera que eran asesinatos. Pero tampoco hizo preguntas capaces de detener la cadena. Hare recibió inmunidad por testificar. Burke fue ejecutado. [short pause] Pero su castigo no terminó en la horca. El hombre que vendía cadáveres para disección terminó sobre una mesa de disección. Y su esqueleto todavía se conserva en Edimburgo.
